slide-0-subtitle:
Product Designer specializing in complex systems,
                        evidence-based design, and systematic scaling.
slide-1-text-0: Education & Background: M.S. in Information Science from UNC Chapel Hill, specializing in Human-Computer Interaction, and a B.A. in Philosophy , minors in Japanese and Creative Writing, from Virginia Tech.
slide-1-text-1: Personal Hobbies: You can usually find me hiking, traveling, reading, or diving down Wikipedia rabbit holes. I love learning!
slide-1-text-2: Evidence-Based Design: I believe the best design decisions are justified. I use data and user insights to validate where a design is going.
slide-1-text-3: Problem Solver: I look beyond the immediate feature request to understand the whole context and establish a good foundation in user perspectives for the design.
slide-1-text-4: Accessibility: Ensure every design I make is usable by all users, and advocating for increments to make that happen is core to my practice.
slide-2-text-0:
Project 1: Case Management Studio

Designing an extendable, modular platform that allows business admins to configure data, forms, workflows, and branding without using any code.
slide-2-text-1:
Designing an extendable, modular platform
                                    that allows business admins to configure
                                    data, forms, workflows, and branding without
                                    using any code.
slide-2-text-2:
Project 2: Design Operations & Scaling Design

Leading the migration from Sketch to Figma, democratizing design knowledge through the UX Chapter, and innovating testing with coded usability study overlays.
slide-2-text-3:
Leading the migration from Sketch to Figma,
                                    democratizing design knowledge through the
                                    UX Chapter, and innovating testing with
                                    coded usability study overlays.
slide-2-text-4:
Q&A & Discussion

Dedicated Q&A following both the case studies.
slide-2-text-5:
Dedicated Q&A following both the case
                                    studies.
slide-3-text-0: What is a "Case"? A generic term for any business lifecycle or process. For a city government, this could be a Permit Request. For a university, it could a student's Major.
slide-3-text-1: What is a "Workflow"? The tasks and automations to complete a case. For a permit, this is Doc Upload → Inspection → Legal Review. For a university major, this is the courses needed to complete a major.
slide-3-text-2: What is a "Task"? An individual, unit of work assigned to a user or system (in the case of automation) (e.g., 'Submit Transcript' or 'Run Document Check').
slide-3-text-3: The Strategy: Establish an easily extendable foundation capable of representing any of these and many more diverse industry workflows out of the box.
slide-4-text-0: Sprawling Monolithic Codebases: Previous solutions had monolithic codebases that made customizations and product upgrades a nightmare, and required continuous design support.
slide-4-text-1: No-Code Admin Controls: Establish a visual builder where non-technical admins can configure their own case types, tasks, automation, and branding.
slide-4-text-2: Easily extendable: Make it both technically and visually easily extendable, with obvious places and guidance to extend based on use case.
slide-4-text-3: Modular Design Strategy: Customers could just take the features they want with the app still looking and feeling great regardless of these choices.
slide-5-title: Where It Started: Connected Onboarding Usability Study
slide-5-text-0: The Precedent Model: Defining what tasks came before intuitively felt like it forced users to think backward, making conditional logic configuration highly error-prone.
slide-5-text-1: Usability Testing: Advocated for customer usability study: 100% of participants struggled with the precedents model and failed conditional configuration.
slide-5-text-2: Redesigned Dependent Grid: Overhauling to a Dependent model with cell-specific validations reduced cognitive load, while flowchart requests established the business case for CMS.
slide-6-title: Studio Solution 1: The Hybrid Workflow Builder
slide-6-text-0: Working Within Platform Limitations: Users expressed a desire for a drag and drop workflow builder.
slide-6-text-1: Platform Constraints: Drag-and-drop was something that could not be done in our low-code environment.
slide-6-text-2:
Three-Pane Design Strategy: I designed a three-pane hybrid visual builder:

Task/Automatin List on the left (with arrows for reordering) + task/automation configurations in the middle + a read-only visual flowchart on the right (real-time verification).
slide-6-text-3:
Task/Automatin List on the left (with arrows
                                    for reordering) + task/automation
                                    configurations in the middle + a read-only
                                    visual flowchart on the right (real-time
                                    verification).
slide-6-text-4: User Validation: Users reported high confidence with this method and felt it was an acceptable solution.
slide-7-text-0: How Automations Fit: Strong stakeholder disagreement on where automations should live.
slide-7-text-1: Card Sort Results: Conducted a card sorting activity with seven participants, showing that users naturally group automation needs by scope (Global vs. Local vs. Workflow).
slide-7-text-2: Hierarchy: Designed a hierarchical inheritance model. Local takes precedence over global, as creators of specific case types have more context and specific needs than more general global rules.
slide-7-text-3: Consistency: This logic matches existing visibility rules for global vs case type specific forms and data types, keeping the user expectations consistent.
slide-8-text-0: Implicit Data Modeling: Adding fields to a form automatically creates the backend data structures.
slide-8-text-1: AI Acceleration: Users can prompt the system (e.g. "Create a dropdown for US States") and secure AI generates the data type and all options automatically.
slide-8-text-2: Adoption & Scale: Currently used in production by major global enterprises, including Porsche, Pfizer, Vanguard, T. Rowe Price, and the Australian Government.
slide-9-text-0: Top Navigation Layout Pivot: Shifted Workspace navigation to a top-nav layout, preserving horizontal screen space for reviews.
slide-9-text-1: Case Summary Aggregation: Centralizes all key information, case description, milestones, watchers, dynamic fields, into a single, consolidated view.
slide-9-text-2: Dynamic Rendering: While the shell remains consistent, the screen dynamically renders case-specific forms and fields defined in Studio.
slide-10-text-0: Contextual Chat: Integrated secure AI chat inside the worker context to query case history, as well as automatically surfacing similar past cases to suggest resolutions.
slide-10-text-1: Intelligent Summarization: Users can instantly summarize lengthy comment threads or complex attached PDFs, extracting key terms.
slide-10-text-2: Prompt Engineering: Personally engineered the prompts for summary generation for comments and documents.
slide-10-text-3: Efficiency: Enables case workers to make rapid, evidence-backed decisions without manual review or context-switching.
slide-11-text-0: Designing for the Public:For the public portal and authenticated external user, pivoted design languages-shifting from high expert density to clear typography, guided bite-sized steps, and generous spacing.
slide-11-text-1: Public Portals: A mobile-first entry point for anonymous users to discover services, find information, and complete guided intake submission flows.
slide-11-text-2: Authenticated Self-Service: The "My Cases" dashboard allows registered external users to track application timelines, upload missing documents, and communicate with workers.
slide-12-text-0: Mobile-First Priority: Reconfigured the public portal and self-service dashboards mobile-first to support public user smartphone use.
slide-12-text-1: Inclusive Design & Accessibility: Pushed for strict WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across all external touchpoints, including generous touch target sizes (minimum 48x48px) and high-contrast typography to ensure usability for all citizens.
slide-13-text-0: Platform Extensibility: CMS is completely agnostic and extensible. Customers can easily plug in custom task types, email templates, and specialized rules.
slide-13-text-1: Solutions Hub Theming: Unified interface for styles, colors, and logos.
slide-13-text-2: Instant Rebranding: Instantly propagate branding across interfaces based on site.
slide-15-title: Evaluating our Design Tools: Sketch vs Figma
slide-15-text-0: Sketch Symbol Frustrations: Our team was using Sketch, which required complex, nested symbol architectures. It was very difficult to use and to maintain as our system grew.
slide-15-text-1: Cross-Platform Collaboration: Customer Success managers and external partners increasingly needed to view our prototypes. Sketch being Mac-only and prototypes being more difficult to access created major blockers for them.
slide-15-text-2: Internal UX Survey: A team survey showed a 9-to-4 preference for Figma. Designers highlighted its cleaner interface, faster prototyping capabilities, and browser-based collaboration.
slide-16-title: The Figma Hackathon: Proving the Concept
slide-16-text-0: Piloting the Transition: To evaluate a shift to Figma, I hosted project to POC Figma during our engineering hackathon, setting up a shared backlog to distribute the component building work across the team.
slide-16-text-1: Rebuilding from the Source: Rather than just importing old Sketch files, we used the actual SAIL design system documentation as our absolute source of truth to rebuild cleanly.
slide-16-text-2: Reducing Component Bloat: The hackathon proved Figma’s variants were vastly superior. We drastically reduced the total number of components needed by using things like booleans instead of diving through endless nested Sketch symbols.
slide-16-text-3: Evidence-Based Proposal: These empirical findings—the survey and the hackathon performance—formed the basis of our formal proposal, securing leadership buy-in and enterprise licenses.
slide-17-text-0: Ease of Use: Designers can simply bring a library asset onto the canvas and easily toggle states (like active/disabled) via a menu, rather than searching through complex Sketch symbol overrides.
slide-17-text-1: Visual-to-Code Alignment: Worked with front-end developers to map Figma variants and properties directly to the parameters in the SAIL codebase.
slide-17-text-2: Auto Layout Parity: Built components with fluid padding, margins, and constraint rules to match browser rendering exactly.
slide-18-text-0: The Library Working Group: Following the hackathon, we established a working group and a structured backlog to manage bug reports and component requests just like software.
slide-18-text-1: Design Branching Workflows: Instituted a strict review pipeline in Figma. Designers build library updates in isolated branches.
slide-18-text-2: Peer-Review Merge Protocol: Every branch is reviewed by other designers for properties and auto Layout structures before merging into the main library.
slide-18-text-3: Regular Component Releases: Shipping regular versioned releases to ensure designers stayed aligned with updates.
slide-19-text-0: Figma Community Release: Led the release of our internal library publicly, allowing external clients and partners to accelerate Appian development.
slide-19-text-1: Integrating Figma MCP (AI): Connected our design library data directly to AI systems using the Model Context Protocol.
slide-19-text-2: Accelerating Development: By loading our exact component structures and constraints into AI contexts, we drastically speed up new component creation and library updates.
slide-20-title: The UX Chapter: Peer-to-Peer Knowledge Sharing
slide-20-text-0: Interactive Workshops: Restructured the monthly internal forum from passive lecture presentations to interactive, designer-led workshops.
slide-20-text-1: Continuous Education: Facilitated 35 sessions since 2023, covering topics like Figma variable updates, design standards, and AI-assisted workflows.
slide-20-text-2: Democratizing Knowledge: Empowered every designer to contribute to the curriculum, elevating the entire team's skills and aligning design practices.
slide-21-text-0: Testing Coded Prototypes: Utilized vibe-coded HTML/JS prototypes for usability testing, allowing earlier and more rigorous evaluation of complex logic.
slide-21-text-1: Bridging the Fidelity Gap: Testing in code captures realistic system behaviors (database rules, validations) that standard design tools cannot simulate.
slide-21-text-2: Participant Context Bottleneck: Noted that participants struggled during testing because instructions and task lists sat in separate documents, causing context-switching.
slide-22-text-0: Plug-and-Play Overlay: Built a lightweight, floating task panel injected directly onto prototype pages, displaying tasks directly in the user's focus.
slide-22-text-1: Moderator-Friendly Shell: Includes task navigation (Next / Done), minimizable controls, and a custom back button simulating browser history navigation.
slide-22-text-2: Empowering the UX Team: Contributed the overlay as a Kiro custom power and standalone script. Other designers can set up structured studies in seconds.
slide-23-subtitle: Design Operations, Figma Libraries, & General Discussion
